coredata.dc:title: Citric acid-based carbon dots: From revealing new insights into their biological properties to demonstrating their enhanced wound healing potential by in vitro and in vivo experiments
coredata.dc:creator: Kasouni, Athanasia I.
Chatzimitakos, Theodoros G.
Troganis, Anastassios N.
Stalikas, Constantine D.
coredata.dc:description: Citric acid, as a carbon precursor, has extensively been considered by researchers to synthesize fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs), which emit strongly in the blue spectral range. In an endeavor to repurpose CNDs for applications other than those until now reported, non-doped, nitrogen-doped, and nitrogen/sulfur co-doped CNDs, were synthesized using citric acid as one of the most exploited carbon sources. This study explored the effect of heteroatom doping on the biological properties of CNDs placing more emphasis on wound healing. The three CND species were examined with respect to cell proliferation/toxicity, cell migration, bactericidal, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties. From the results, it became apparent that the N,S-doped CNDs exhibit remarkable bactericidal properties, while the N-doped CNDs exhibit the best anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Based on it, in-vitro antioxidant experiments were carried out for the N-doped CNDs, which validated our findings. Next, the N-doped CNDs were evaluated for their wound healing potency by carrying out relevant in-vivo experiments. The results showed that wounds treated with the CNDs were healed four days faster than control wounds. This study provides valuable insights into inspiring new advancements of CNDs and draws a roadmap towards their broader range of biomedical applications underlining the potency in wound healing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Wound healing
Anti-inflammatory
Antioxidant properties
In-vivo experiments

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties of CNDs
Effect of CNDs on viability and migration of eukaryotic cells
Bactericidal properties of CNDs against Staphylococcus aureus
In vitro assessment of oxidative stress protection properties of CNDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Percentage of viability of cells not treated with hydrogen peroxide or N-doped CNDs (negative control), cells treated for 5 h only with hydrogen peroxide (control cells), and cells treated for 5 h with hydrogen peroxide after 24 h of incubation with N-doped CNDs (samples).
Percentage of decreased concentration of ROS in cells (caused by the addition of H2O2) after incubation with N-doped CNDs, compared with control cells (considered as 100 % ROS concentration).

Percentage of cells (%) in different phases of the cell cycle for negative control (cells not treated with hydrogen peroxide or N-doped CNDs), control (cells treated for 5 h only with hydrogen peroxide), and sample (cells treated for 5 h with hydrogen peroxide after 24 h incubation with N-doped CNDs).
Representative photographs of wounds on days 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 from rats treated with a cream containing 0 (control), 1, 2, and 4 mg g−1 N-doped CNDs.
Graphical representation of the % wound closure on different days post wound creation, upon treatment with a cream containing (A) 1, (B) 2, and (C) 4 mg g−1 N-doped CNDs.

head.ce:title: Citric acid-based carbon dots: From revealing new insights into their biological properties to demonstrating their enhanced wound healing potential by in vitro and in vivo experiments

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Biophysical Chemistry, Department of Biological Applications and Technologies, University of Ioannina, 45110, Ioannina, Greece

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biological Applications and Technologies

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Biophysical Chemistry, Department of Biological Applications and Technologies, University of Ioannina, 45110, Ioannina, Greece

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, University of Ioannina, 45110 Ioannina, Greece
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, University of Ioannina, 45110 Ioannina, Greece

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Citric acid, as a carbon precursor, has extensively been considered by researchers to synthesize fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs), which emit strongly in the blue spectral range. In an endeavor to repurpose CNDs for applications other than those until now reported, non-doped, nitrogen-doped, and nitrogen/sulfur co-doped CNDs, were synthesized using citric acid as one of the most exploited carbon sources. This study explored the effect of heteroatom doping on the biological properties of CNDs placing more emphasis on wound healing. The three CND species were examined with respect to cell proliferation/toxicity, cell migration, bactericidal, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties. From the results, it became apparent that the N,S-doped CNDs exhibit remarkable bactericidal properties, while the N-doped CNDs exhibit the best anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Based on it, in-vitro antioxidant experiments were carried out for the N-doped CNDs, which validated our findings. Next, the N-doped CNDs were evaluated for their wound healing potency by carrying out relevant in-vivo experiments. The results showed that wounds treated with the CNDs were healed four days faster than control wounds. This study provides valuable insights into inspiring new advancements of CNDs and draws a roadmap towards their broader range of biomedical applications underlining the potency in wound healing.

ce:section.ce:para: The idea of using nanomaterials in biomedical research has drawn much attention and interest due to the fact that nanoparticles can easily be engineered to attain unparalleled functions and unique compositions, thus providing novel tools and techniques useful for biological research [1]. The advanced and unique characteristics of the synthesized nanomaterials, used as tools for widespread application in biomedicine, could be attributed to their typically minuscule size. The nano-sized materials have the inherent potential to bypass the cellular and subcellular barriers and interact closely with the important cellular and sub-cellular components including DNA, proteins, and lipid-containing cell membrane thereby modulating the physiological procedures. Their interactions with the living cells and biomolecules serve as precursors for various biological effects that can be either beneficial or detrimental to human health.

Lately, nanomaterials have been employed in health-related applications to enhance existing therapies or develop new alternative treatments. For this reason, nanomaterials are examined in terms of their antioxidant, antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, etc. properties which can be used for the treatment of various disorders [2–5]. Among others, it is an undeniable fact that the treatment of wounds is a growing healthcare challenge, worldwide [6–8]. Not only the treatment of wounds is difficult, but failing to heal, normally, can lead to chronic wounds which are in a state of pathologic inflammation that further hinders wound healing [9]. Healing of chronic wounds is a heavy economic burden for healthcare systems ($25 billion are spent annually in the US) and cause discomfort to patients [10]. Instead of trying to heal chronic wounds, it is more reasonable to avoid their formation. This can be done by reducing the time needed for a wound to heal.

The success of nanomaterials in healing is attributed to the multiple actions (e.g. antibacterial and hemostatic or anti-inflammatory and proliferation stimulation) and reduction of off-site side effects [11,12]. Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are a relatively novel class of carbon nanomaterials, distinguished from the rest of carbon nanomaterials by their exceptional chemical and photoluminescent properties [13,14]. More and more researchers have been trying to unravel the full potential CNDs, in several fields of science. A significant key-merit of CNDs is their negligible cytotoxicity towards eukaryotic cells. This property made feasible their use in biological applications, such as in vitro and in vivo bioimaging, drug, and gene delivery, and as carriers that can penetrate the blood-brain barrier [15–17]. However, up to now, CNDs have been poorly studied with regard to their wound-healing abilities. For this reason, there are scanty and sparse reports about the wound healing potential of certain CNDs. Bankoti et al. prepared CNDs from onion peel powder [18] and wounds treated with them were healed by ∼15 % more, compared with control wounds, after two weeks. This effect was attributed to enhanced fibroblast migration. In our laboratory, we prepared CNDs from human fingernails, following a carbonization procedure [19]. The CNDs stimulated cell migration, and cell proliferation (an 18 % increase in the number of HEK-293 was recorded after 48 h). The latter effect was achieved by enhancing the transition of the cell cycle from phase S to G2/M.

All the above studies provide an impetus to the research of CNDs in wound healing applications. Considering that no study has examined thoroughly this topic, our aim herein is to explore the doping effect on wound healing and other biological properties of CNDs. Repurposed citric acid-based CNDs were synthesized along with their nitrogen-doped (N-doped) and nitrogen and sulfur co-doped (N,S-doped) analogs using urea and thiourea as nitrogen and nitrogen/sulfur source, respectively. The citric acid as a carbon precursor has extensively been considered to synthesize fluorescent CNDs, which emit strongly in the blue spectral range. The first report on the reaction between citric acid and urea at 130 °C, dates back to Sell and Easterfield, in 1893, who studied the formation of the blue fluorescent citrazinic acid [20]. In our research, citric acid was selected because (i) it is one of the most exploited pure chemical precursors for CNDs and (ii) the biological properties and wound healing potency of citric acid-based CNDs have been unexplored until now. Properties, commonly associated with wound healing (i.e. antioxidant activity, anti-inflammatory, bactericidal activity, cell proliferation, and cell migration properties) were studied for the selected CNDs to (i) shed light on the heteroatom doping on wound healing, and (ii) show their promising attributes as new-generation biological factors. After selecting the most promising of the CNDs species, in vivo experiments were carried out and the wound healing potential was further highlighted.
All information regarding the reagents, solutions, and instruments used in this study are given in detail in the Supplementary material.

The CNDs employed in this work were synthesized according to previous procedures [21–23]. In brief, for the synthesis of the non-doped CNDs, 0.20 g of citric acid was heated in an autoclave at 200 °C, for 3 h. The N-doped CNDs were synthesized by dissolving 0.13 g of citric acid and 0.11 g of urea in 3 mL of double-distilled water so that a transparent solution was formed. The solution was heated in an autoclave at 160 °C, for 8 h. The N,S-doped CNDs were synthesized by homogenizing in a mortar 0.10 g of citric acid and 0.11 g of thiourea. The mixture was placed in an autoclave and heated at 200 °C, for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, water was added to the viscous liquids to dissolve them. After centrifuging at 6000 rpm for 5 min, the pH was adjusted to 7.0, using appropriate volumes of HCl or NaOH solutions. Then, the neutralized solutions were dialyzed against water for 24 h (changing water every 2−4 h) using a benzoylated dialysis tubing membrane (MWCO: 2000 KDa) and finally freeze-dried. The obtained CNDs were stored in the dark. More experimental details are given in the Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties of CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antioxidant activity of the CNDs was assessed using the DPPH free radical scavenging assay, reducing power assay, inhibition of lipid peroxidation (TBARS) assay, hydrogen peroxide scavenging assay, metal chelating activity, and inhibition of protein oxidation assay. The anti-inflammatory properties of the CNDs were assessed by the albumin denaturation assay and the red blood cell membrane stabilization assay. The experimental procedures are given in the Supplementary material.
Blood compatibility was examined by studying the hemolysis of red blood cells and the blood clot time. Details are given in the Supplementary material.
Information on the antibacterial activity assessment of CNDs is given in the Supplementary material.
Details regarding the in vitro experiments (i.e. cell viability, scratch assay) and the oxidative stress protection properties of CNDs are provided in the Supplementary material.

For the in vivo experiments, fifteen male Wistar rats, weighing between 200 and 220 g each, were used. The animals were maintained at 22 ± 2 °C in a 12 h light - 12 h dark cycle. Food and water were provided to animals ad libitum. The experiments were approved by the institutional animal ethical committee (University of Ioannina, Greece, Protocol No 2757/22.03.2019) and were conducted in accordance with the internationally accepted principles for laboratory animal use and care (EEC Directive of 1986; 86/609/EEC).

Animals were anesthetized by ketamine injection (60 mg/kg) intraperitoneally. Ketamine acted both as a sedative and as a long-term pain killer [24]. Under anesthesia, dorsal hair was shaved and the area was disinfected using 70 % alcohol solution. Two identical full-thickness dorsal wounds (diameter 8 mm) were created, one on each side of the depilated dorsum of each rat, using a skin biopsy punch.

Of the two wounds on each rat, one served as the control and the second as the test wound. On the control and the test wound, a dose of hydrophilic aqueous base cream (Chemco) and a dose of a cream containing the CNDs at various concentrations were applied, respectively. The dose was ∼100 mg per wound and one dose per day was applied in all rats. The rat skin was inspected on a daily basis and photographs were taken from a fixed distance. The areas of the wounds were calculated using the Image J software. The wound closure (%) was calculated using the following equation:
%
W o u n d
c l o s u r e =
A o
- A
A o
× 100
where Ao and A are the areas of the wound from control and sample, respectively [25].

To ensure reproducibility, all the assays were repeated three times. Statistical significance was considered for P values lower than 0.05. The levels of significance between samples were compared by the one-way ANOVA using Student's t-test, as a post-hoc test for comparison of means. The results were expressed as mean ± standard deviation.

The CNDs were synthesized based on different experimental conditions (i.e. temperature and time); it was found that the properties of dots (vide infra) were not significantly affected. On the contrary, the originally reported conditions of synthesis maximized the yield of CNDs. Information regarding the synthesis modifications and characterization of the final three CNDs is given in detail in the Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CNDs on viability and migration of eukaryotic cells
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of CNDs on the viability of two eukaryotic cell lines (i.e. HEK-293 and HaCaT) was evaluated. The cell viability was reduced by less than 10 % after 48 h of incubation with up to 400 μg mL−1 of either of non-doped, N-doped, and N,S-doped CNDs (Figs. S4-S6 of the Supplementary material). No statistically significant difference (p > 0.05) was observed among the three CNDs species, bespeaking no alteration of the cytotoxicity of the citric acid-based CNDs due to doping. The CNDs were also evaluated in terms of their effect on the migration of the two aforementioned cell lines. None of the CNDs, at any tested concentration was able to promote or hinder cell migration. Based on the above, the three CNDs species are safe to be used for biological applications up to the studied concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bactericidal properties of CNDs against Staphylococcus aureus
ce:section.ce:para: The N,S-doped CNDs reduced the number of viable S. aureus cells by 70 %, after 4 h of incubation with 600 μg mL−1 (Figure S7 of the Supplementary material). Under the same conditions, N-doped CNDs and non-doped CNDs reduced the number of cells by 40 % and 10 %, respectively.

The potential of CNDs to inhibit protein denaturation was examined by studying the heat-induced denaturation of albumin in the presence and absence of CNDs and the stabilization of human red blood cell membrane. As evidenced by Figures S8 and S9 of the Supplementary material, non-doped CNDs can scarcely exhibit any anti-inflammatory activity, in both assays, since their activity is less than 20 %. The N,S-doped CNDs exhibited a slightly higher anti-inflammatory activity compared with non-doped CNDs, but still, they were not satisfactory to be put to use for wound healing. Contrarily, N-doped CNDs exhibited exceptional anti-inflammatory properties. Even the lowest tested concentration (25 μg mL−1) was able to inhibit albumin denaturation by 55 % and to stabilize red blood cell membrane by 40 %. At the highest tested concentration of the N-doped CNDs (200 μg mL−1), the respective activities recorded were 97 % and 82 %.

First, the potential of CNDs to scavenge free DPPH radicals was explored. Figure S10 of Supplementary material shows the percentage of scavenging of DPPH free radicals at various concentrations of the three CNDs and the BHT (a reference antioxidant compound). While the non-doped CNDs were completely incapable of scavenging DPPH free radicals at all tested concentrations (observed maximum scavenging: 3.8 %), both doped CNDs were efficient radical scavengers. Between the two doped CNDs, the N-doped were found to be superior to N,S-doped CNDs, since 100 % scavenging was achieved with 0.2 mg mL−1 by the former compared with 1.0 mg mL−1 by the latter. Surprisingly enough, the N-doped CNDs were even superior to BHT as a free radical scavenger. The EC50 values (the concentration of a compound that scavenges 50 % of the free radicals) were found to be 0.275 mg mL−1 and 0.016 mg mL−1 for N,S-doped, and N-doped CNDs, respectively. Similar results were obtained in the case of using the hydrogen peroxide assay, instead of the DPPH (Figure S11 of Supplementary material). N-doped CNDs exhibited excellent scavenging activity, achieving 100 % scavenging at 0.4 mg mL−1. The other two CNDs species exhibited significantly lower scavenging activity (0.4 mg mL−1 of N,S doped CNDs, and non-doped CNDs exhibit ∼30 % and ∼15 % scavenging activity, respectively).

Reducing power is another important assay that can serve as an indicator of antioxidant activity [26]. From Fig. S12 of Supplementary material, it is obvious that the non-doped CNDs do not exhibit any reducing power, at all tested concentrations. The doping of the CNDs with nitrogen greatly increased this power, while the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CNDs exhibited a moderate property. It was observed that the reducing power of N,S-doped CNDs was about 50 % lower than that of N-doped CNDs, at all concentrations studied. Furthermore, 0.2 mg mL−1 of N-doped CNDs exhibited satisfactory reducing power and hence antioxidant activity. All the materials exhibited reducing power lower than that of gallic acid, which was used as a reference reducing compound. Furthermore, the N-doped CNDs were better metal chelators compared with the two other kinds of CNDs, as can be seen in Figure S13 of Supplementary material. Finally, the performance of CNDs to inhibit protein oxidation and the formation of TBARS was evaluated (Figures S14 and S15 of Supplementary material). In both assays, N-doped CNDs could more effectively protect proteins from oxidation, than the two other CNDs; this is in accordance with the above results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro assessment of oxidative stress protection properties of CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on all the results presented above, the N-doped CNDs exhibit the best antioxidant activity, compared with the other two CND species. Therefore, further experiments for the protection of cells from oxidative stress were carried out only with N-doped CNDs.

HaCaT and HEK-293 cells were used to assess the oxidative damages caused by short-lived hydroxyl radicals in the presence and absence of the N-doped CNDs. The cell viability was examined 24 h post-treatment of cells with hydrogen peroxide (for 5 h) and it was found to be 51 ± 1% and 54 ± 1% for HaCaT and HEK-293 cells, respectively (Fig. 1 ). The viability of cells treated with 100 μg mL−1 of N-doped CNDs prior to the exposure to hydroxyl radicals was found to be ～80 % for both cell lines (i.e. 30 % higher than the control cells), while a concentration of 200 μg mL−1 further increased cell viability to ～90 %, which was a significant improvement. In addition, the morphology of cells was improved, compared with control cells (Fig. S16 of Supplementary material). Further increase in the concentration of the N-doped CNDs did not increase cell viability. This was due to a minor decrease in viability, which N-doped induced at a concentration of 200 μg mL−1. From these results, it was demonstrated that N-doped CNDs can effectively protect cells from oxidative stress.

Aside from the hydroxyl radicals examined, other reactive oxygen species (ROS) may be formed and co-exist in the cells. For both cell lines, cells treated only with hydrogen peroxide were considered to constitute the control and the absorbance measured at the end of the assay was taken as 100 % oxidative stress. When cells were incubated with 100 μg mL−1 before the addition of hydrogen peroxide, ROS were found to decrease by nearly 20 % for both cell lines. When 200 μg mL−1 of N-doped CNDs were used, the ROS concentration decreased by 60 % for HaCaT cells and 45 % for HEK-293 cells. In the case of 400 μg mL−1 of N-doped CNDs, the ROS concentration decreased by 48 % for HaCaT cells and 42 % for HEK-293 cells. These values are lower than those at the concentration of 200 μg mL−1 N-doped CNDs, thus justifying the reduced viability of cells, as can be seen in Fig. 2 . It is evident that N-doped CNDs can effectively protect cells from oxidative stress and reduce the intracellular concentration of ROS. As a result, they are expected to assist in wound healing.

To further validate the results which showed that the cell viability under oxidative stress was higher for cells treated with N- doped CDNs, we examined the cell cycles of these cells using flow cytometry. As can be seen in Fig. 3 , the control sample (treated with hydrogen peroxide) compared with the negative control sample (cells not treated with hydrogen peroxide) exhibited a higher percentage of cells in the sub-G1, S, and the G2/M phase and a lower percentage of cells in the G0/G1 phase. When cells were pre-incubated with the N-doped CNDs, it was obvious that the overall distribution of cells in the four phases was more similar to that of the negative control cells. The best protection effect from oxidative stress was achieved when cells were treated with 200 μg mL−1 of N-doped CNDs. Specifically, the percentage of HaCaT cells in sub-G1, S, and G2/M phases was lower by 6.6 %, 2.4 %, and 2.0 %, respectively, compared with the control cells, while the percentage of cells in the G0/G1 phase was higher by 11.2 % compared with the control. Similar results were observed for HEK-293 cells. Although the treatment with 100 μg mL-1 was not such efficient as the 200 μg mL−1 it was obvious that even a small concentration of N-doped CNDs could decrease the percentage of dead HaCaT cells (sub-G1 phase) by ∼45 % (decrease from 9.6 % to 5.3 %), highlighting the protective effect of the CNDs. This decrease in cell number in the sub-G1 phase was even more pronounced in the case of HEK-293 cells, whose percentage was decreased by 66 % compared with control cells.

The compatibility of N-doped CNDs with blood was examined following a hemolysis assay. Up to a concentration of 400 μg mL−1, no hemolysis was observed, bespeaking blood compatibility of the examined CNDs. Additionally, the effect of CNDs on the blood clot time was studied. The N-doped CNDs neither increased nor decreased the time that blood needed to form clots, up to a concentration of 400 μg mL−1, signifying good compatibility.

Assessing the biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of CNDs before considering their biological application is important for any further advancement, which includes in vitro and in vivo tests [19]. From our results, all three CNDs species were found suitable for biological applications, as their cytotoxicity towards eukaryotic cells was found to be less than 10 %. In a previous study, we found that N,S-doped CNDs from human nails are able to promote cell proliferation [19], which is not the case for the CNDs examined herein. Another parameter often examined in wound healing applications is the effect of a factor on cell migration, which is of prime importance for the development of α new tissue [27]. In a previous study, we found that CNDs from the carbonization of human fingernails can promote the migration of HEK-293 cells [19]. Generally, the enhancement of cell proliferation and the promotion of cell migration are highly desired properties. According to our results, none of the tested CNDs is able to promote cell migration. Although this does not support the use of CNDs as wound healing agents, the fact that CNDs do not hinder cell migration can still be considered important for such an application since such an unwanted side-effect is avoided. Our results showed that doping of CNDs with heteroatoms does not bestow them with such properties (although other properties are altered) and that other characteristics of the CNDs may be responsible for such properties.

A parameter that can delay or even stop the wound healing process of injured tissues is bacterial infections [28,29]. Thus, counteracting bacterial infections, or even better preventing them can benefit the wound healing process [30]. Materials with antibacterial properties can assist in the overall wound healing process. Despite the fact that doping of CNDs with heteroatoms had no effect on their toxicity towards eukaryotic cells, this was not the case for prokaryotic cells. In our study, S. aureus was selected as a model organism, because it interferes with the wound healing process and the combat of its infections is more difficult compared with infections from other gram-negative species [31]. Our results demonstrated that doping of CNDs with nitrogen increased their antibacterial activity against S. aureus, while nitrogen and sulfur co-doping increased even more that. These results are in accordance with our previous study, where the N,S-doped CNDs were found to be more potent antibacterial agents than N-doped and non-doped CNDs [32]. In another study, Omidi et al. synthesized N-doped CNDs from ammonium hydrogen citrate [33] and they found that 10 μg mL−1 of CNDs can totally inhibit the growth of S. aureus. Similarly, Muktha et al. produced CNDs from sucrose, glucose, or fructose and found that they exhibit antibacterial activity at low concentrations (25−50 μg mL−1) [34]. In both studies, the authors proposed that the developed CNDs can be used for wound healing, by virtue of the antibacterial activity of the CNDs. In our case, CNDs exhibit low to moderate antibacterial activity and as such, this activity can be considered complementary to the other ones.

Inflammation is a critical step in the wound healing process. The denaturation of proteins and the lysosomal membrane destabilization are two well-established causes of inflammation, among others [35,36]. By limiting the extent of them, the inflammation is also limited, leading to faster wound healing. The ability of CNDs to do that and stabilize the membrane of human red blood cells (since their membranes are highly analogous to the membranes of lysosomes) were examined [35]. Our results demonstrated that the doping of CNDs with heteroatoms affects their anti-inflammatory potential and nitrogen-doping bestow excellent anti-inflammatory properties on CNDs. This is in accordance with previous studies reporting that the presence of amide groups in a compound enhances its anti-inflammatory properties [37,38].

It is known that during the inflammatory phase of wound healing, the amount of free radicals at the wound site is increased [39]. Free radicals cause multiple damages to cellular components, such as cell membranes, lipids, proteins, DNA, etc., and as a result, cell mortality and oxidative stress can occur, delaying the wound healing process [40,41]. Thus, it is expected that if CNDs possess antioxidant properties they will be more potent for wound healing. As the oxidative damages are complex within an organism multiple assays were employed to obtain a more complete overview of the antioxidant properties. Although DPPH is an artificial radical and it is not expected to exist at the wound sites, using DPPH to evaluate the antioxidant properties of compounds or mixtures is an established procedure that can serve as a benchmark for other assays [42]. The results were conclusive that N-doped CNDs exhibited better free-radical scavenging properties than those of the other CND species. In a recent study, it was demonstrated that the presence of carboxyl-and amino-groups increase the free-radical scavenging activity of CNDs, with the latter being even more favorable [43]. Oxidative damage can also occur from metal-catalyzed reactions. For instance, the Fenton reaction causes faster cell apoptosis [44] compared with free radicals. Therefore, inhibiting the process of such reactions would be beneficial for wound healing. The metal chelating activity of the CNDs can also contribute to their antibacterial properties and thus, enhance their wound healing potential. This is because metals, such as iron, are essential for bacteria reproduction. Therefore, if metals are withdrawn from the wound site, bacteria reproduction can be delayed, resulting in bacteriostasis, a mechanism of action similar to that of the lactoferrin protein [45]. Our results were conclusive that N-doped CND species were superior in terms of metal chelating activity and reducing power, over the two other CND species. According to all the above, since N-doped CNDs can combine both good anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties and minor bactericidal properties, they were selected as the best option among the three examined CNDs to be used for wound healing applications. Although N,S-doped CNDs exhibit better bactericidal properties, their moderate antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, make them less suitable for wound healing compared to N-doped CNDs. Combating bacterial infections is a complementary activity that avoids the disruption of wound healing by bacteria, and does not directly affect the overall wound healing process, as in the case of antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity.

Before conducting in vivo experiments, it is good practice to ensure that the tested compounds will pose no threat to the animals and will not cause unwanted side effects. At the wound site, there is excess blood due to damaged blood vessels. Therefore, applying a treatment will result in interactions with blood. The N-doped CNDs were found to be compatible with red blood cells and thus, further damage is avoided.

The in vivo wound healing experiments undoubtedly supported our previous finding, that N-doped CNDs are good candidates for wound healing applications. From our results, it was apparent that in the course of time the difference in the wound closure between control and sample decreased. This is because CNDs acted at the first stages of the wound healing process (by reducing the oxidative stress of the cells and inflammation and preventing bacterial infections), assisting in their completion in a much faster manner than that in the control wounds. Subsequently, their effect decreased, and the wound healing progresses with a rate more similar to that of the control wound. All the above findings strengthen the notion that doping of citric acid-based CNDs with heteroatoms bestows different properties on them and that doping with nitrogen is the most suitable to produce CNDs with advanced wound healing properties. Apart from the wound healing, the other biological properties of the studied CNDs are noteworthy. This is because the anti-inflammatory properties of the CNDs can be potentially be exploited to develop a treatment for other disorders where inflammatory processes occur, such as chronic inflammatory bowel disease, neurodegenerative diseases, etc. [3]. Likewise, the antioxidant properties of CNDs can be reaped for the treatment of diseases, such as cardiovascular diseases, multiple sclerosis, etc. [4,5].

This work offers an overview of some biological and biomedical properties of CNDs, usually synthesized for other applications. Relevant tests were performed to provide a complete framework for each kind of CNDs. The results demonstrate that the doping of CNDs with heteroatoms can significantly affect their potential for wound healing applications. This is because the N-doped, and N,S-doped CNDs exhibit bactericidal, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory properties, which are dependent on their chemical structure, whereas the non-doped species exhibit almost no activity. Between the two doped CNDs, the N-doped exhibit outstanding antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, making them excellent candidates for wound healing applications. Relying on their bactericidal properties, low cytotoxicity, and their blood compatibility, N-doped CNDs were used in in vivo experiments. The results clearly demonstrate their suitability and validate that their activity manifests itself mainly on the first days of the wound healing process. Owing to this, the formation of chronic wounds can be avoided since the inflammatory phase is completed at a reasonable rate. Our results clearly indicate that doping with heteroatoms alters the wound healing potential of CNDs and that doping with nitrogen is more suitable to develop CNDs for wound healing applications. Although more aspects of CND effects on wound healing should be studied, this study can serve as a useful reference point to benchmark future studies that will focus on the wound healing properties of the CNDs, an emerging field of research.

Athanasia I. Kasouni: Investigation, Conceptualization, Writing - original draft. Theodoros G. Chatzimitakos: Investigation, Conceptualization, Writing - original draft. Anastassios N. Troganis: Data curation, Methodology. Constantine D. Stalikas: Supervision, Methodology, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks are due to the Laboratory of Immunology of Biological Applications and Technology Department of the University of Ioannina, for providing access to their facilities. The authors would like also to thank Prof. Leonardos I. for providing access to the facilities of the Laboratory of Zoology (Biological Applications and Technology Department of the University of Ioannina) and Ph.D. candidate Chousidis J. for his assistance in the recording of the photographs with the optical microscope. The authors are grateful to Panagiotis Lekkas, for his assistance in the in vivo experiments.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtcomm.2021.102019.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
